comment_text.sn4.20:1.3: Legend has it that the Buddha’s destiny was either to be Awakened or become a Wheel Turning Monarch. If he took the second path, he would indeed have ruled without violence (MN 91:5.8). Here the Buddha wonders whether this is really possible.
comment_text.sn4.20:2.2: Māra knows that even the most righteous ruler is afflicted with compromises and remains within his realm.

root_text.sn4.20:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kosalesu viharati himavantapadese araññakuṭikāyaṁ.
root_text.sn4.20:1.2: Atha kho bhagavato rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn4.20:1.3: “sakkā nu kho rajjaṁ kāretuṁ ahanaṁ aghātayaṁ ajinaṁ ajāpayaṁ asocaṁ asocāpayaṁ dhammenā”ti?
root_text.sn4.20:2.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā bhagavato cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn4.20:2.2: “kāretu, bhante, bhagavā rajjaṁ, kāretu, sugato, rajjaṁ ahanaṁ aghātayaṁ ajinaṁ ajāpayaṁ asocaṁ asocāpayaṁ dhammenā”ti.
root_text.sn4.20:2.3: “Kiṁ pana me tvaṁ, pāpima, passasi yaṁ maṁ tvaṁ evaṁ vadesi:
root_text.sn4.20:2.4: ‘kāretu, bhante, bhagavā rajjaṁ, kāretu sugato, rajjaṁ ahanaṁ aghātayaṁ ajinaṁ ajāpayaṁ asocaṁ asocāpayaṁ dhammenā’”ti?
root_text.sn4.20:2.5: “Bhagavatā kho, bhante, cattāro iddhipādā bhāvitā bahulīkatā yānīkatā vatthukatā anuṭṭhitā paricitā susamāraddhā.
root_text.sn4.20:2.6: Ākaṅkhamāno ca, bhante, bhagavā himavantaṁ pabbatarājaṁ suvaṇṇaṁ tveva adhimucceyya suvaṇṇañca panassā”ti.
root_text.sn4.20:5.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ bhagavā, jānāti maṁ sugato”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn4.20:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kosalans, in a wilderness hut on the slopes of the Himalayas.
translation_text.sn4.20:1.2: Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind,
translation_text.sn4.20:1.3: “I wonder if it’s possible to rule legitimately, without killing or having someone kill for you; without conquering or having someone conquer for you; without sorrowing or causing sorrow?”
translation_text.sn4.20:2.1: And then Māra the Wicked, knowing the Buddha’s train of thought, went up to him and said,
translation_text.sn4.20:2.2: “Rule, Blessed One! Rule, Holy One! Rule legitimately, without killing or having someone kill for you; without conquering or having someone conquer for you; without sorrowing or causing sorrow!”
translation_text.sn4.20:2.3: “But what do you see, Wicked One, that you say this to me?”
translation_text.sn4.20:2.5: “The Blessed One, sir, has developed and cultivated the four bases for psychic power, made them a vehicle and a basis, kept them up, consolidated them, and properly implemented them.
translation_text.sn4.20:2.6: If he wished, the Blessed One need only determine that the Himalaya, king of mountains, was gold, and it would turn into gold.”
translation_text.sn4.20:3.2: entirely of native gold, and double it—
translation_text.sn4.20:3.3: it’s still not enough for one!
translation_text.sn4.20:3.4: Knowing this, live a moral life.
translation_text.sn4.20:4.1: When a personage has seen where suffering comes from
translation_text.sn4.20:4.2: how could they incline towards sensual pleasures?
translation_text.sn4.20:4.3: Realizing that attachment is a chain in the world,
translation_text.sn4.20:4.4: a personage would train to remove it.”
translation_text.sn4.20:5.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.

variant_text.sn4.20:2.6: suvaṇṇañca panassā”ti → suvaṇṇapabbatassāti (bj, sya-all, km); suvaṇṇañca pabbatassāti (pts1ed, pts2ed)
